coaching.coaching: Weigh at least one structural alternative for hard gate implementation mechanism options.
coaching.subject: hard gate implementation mechanism options

coaching.coaching: Pin down AC1 allowlist removal verification conditions with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: AC1 allowlist removal verification conditions

coaching.coaching: Plan how acceptance criteria completeness and consistency will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria completeness and consistency

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for architecture.md cap semantics after Phase 2 — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: architecture.md cap semantics after Phase 2

coaching.coaching: Propagate the revision for documentation update scope across child stories to the child tickets.
coaching.subject: documentation update scope across child stories

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the limit-lock gate (e.g. a single canonical constant file from which both the Python test and shell gate read, with a drift gate detecting any divergence) and add a positive rationale for that choice; also define what 'admin manual override' means operationally (e.g. which file/step an admin edits and how that change bypasses the gate).
evidence: SOFT_CAP = 800  # docs/architecture.md 'Module-size policy' — must match the CI gate's threshold.  (tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py:37)
Shell gate: $1 > 800 (gerrit-verify.yaml:188 and test.yml:232) — a second, independent encoding of the threshold not guarded by any existing lock
ADR 0038: 'We deliberately do not add a CODEOWNERS file' — no per-file protection mechanism exists in the repo
infra/github/apply-mirror-lock.sh: mirror lock restricts pushes to main (non-deploy-key actors), not per-file changes within a normally-landed Gerrit change
ABSENCE: the plan never names the mechanism (drift gate, Gerrit submit rule, CI sentinel, pinned external canonical) nor provides a positive rationale for any choice
findings.finding: The 'limit-lock' hard gate in Phase 2 has no specified mechanism and no positive rationale. The 800-LOC threshold lives in two independent loci (SOFT_CAP = 800 in tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py and $1 > 800 in shell within gerrit-verify.yaml + test.yml); the plan does not say which loci to guard, what CI/Gerrit mechanism enforces the lock, or what 'admin manual override' means operationally given that the project explicitly has no CODEOWNERS (ADR 0038) and the mirror-lock only restricts non-deploy-key pushes to main rather than providing per-file protection.
findings.location: Goal section ('add a hard gate so the module-size limit itself cannot be changed through the normal contribution process') and AC #3 ('A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required); documented')
scenarios: An implementer adds a CI drift gate checking only the Python constant but leaves the shell threshold unguarded — a contributor can silently raise the cap to 900 in the two YAML files without failing any gate

An implementer chooses a Gerrit submit rule (unknown to the codebase) while another would choose a drift gate — the two approaches have different operational (admin override) meanings and neither is canonically wrong, so the plan gives no basis for a coherent choice

The 'admin manual override required' clause is unactionable if the override path is not specified — does it mean editing a pinned file with a special token? Disabling a CI job? Running an admin script?

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the 'hard gate' that locks the 800-LOC limit (e.g., Gerrit submit rule, GitHub file-path ruleset, out-of-band constant), verify that the chosen mechanism genuinely requires an admin action to bypass given the free-org constraints documented in ADR 0011, and confirm all locations where the constant 800 lives are covered.
evidence: Plan AC #3: 'A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required); documented'
ADR 0011: 'a trusted repo admin can deliberately edit/disable the rule — an explicitly accepted PoC risk' (free GitHub org limitation)
ADR 0038: 'We deliberately do not add a CODEOWNERS file' — no per-file ownership enforcement exists
test_module_size_contract.py:37: SOFT_CAP = 800 — the constant is a plain Python literal modifiable in a normal change
.github/workflows/test.yml:232 and gerrit-verify.yaml:188: 800 is also hardcoded in shell awk expressions — no single protected source of truth
Absence: no existing 'admin-only' file or config-lock pattern is cited or identified in the plan
findings.finding: The plan asserts a novel 'hard gate' that makes the 800-LOC limit unchangeable through the normal contribution process (requiring an 'admin manual override') without identifying any implementation mechanism or verifying that the available infrastructure can actually deliver this property. ADR 0011 explicitly acknowledges that on a free GitHub org, even ruleset-level protections can be edited/disabled by a trusted repo admin — true 'admin-proof' enforcement is an accepted risk. ADR 0038 deliberately omits CODEOWNERS. The closest existing pattern (mypy ratchet test) can be modified in the same normal-contribution Gerrit change. The 800-LOC constant appears in three locations (SOFT_CAP in the test, hardcoded in two workflow shell scripts). No mechanism (e.g., Gerrit submit rule, GitHub file-level ruleset, out-of-band config) is named, and no prior art for 'limit-lock' patterns in this infrastructure is considered.
findings.location: AC #3 / Phase 2 / 'add a hard gate so the module-size limit itself cannot be changed through the normal contribution process (an admin must manually override)'
scenarios: An implementer must independently choose between Gerrit submit rules (requires Gerrit admin access and Prolog/CEL knowledge), a GitHub branch ruleset scoped to a specific file path (requires a paid org for admin-proof enforcement), a CI test that reads a committed constant (modifiable in the same change), or some other approach — the choice materially affects whether the gate actually works.
If the mechanism chosen is a CI test or contract test, a contributor can modify both the limit and the test in the same Gerrit change and get a Verified +1 — providing no protection.
The 800 constant currently appears in at least three files; a 'lock' on only one of them leaves the others unprotected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the 'hard gate' that prevents changing the 800-LOC limit through a normal contribution (e.g., a Gerrit submit-requirement that checks the constant's value, a GitHub repository ruleset that requires admin bypass for specific files, or an admin-only ref ACL on the file containing the limit), noting that CODEOWNERS is ruled out by ADR-0038 and a CI-script grep is self-defeating.
evidence: AC3 text: 'A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required); documented'
ADR-0038 (docs/adr/0038-governance-artifacts.md): 'We deliberately do not add a CODEOWNERS file, nor enable Gerrit's code-owners plugin' — rules out the natural per-file protection mechanism
infra/gerrit/project.config: ACLs are per-ref (refs/heads/*, refs/for/*), not per-file within a change — Gerrit does not natively enforce per-file change restrictions

No mechanism named in the plan: could be a CI assertion on the hardcoded constant, a GitHub file-level branch protection rule, a Gerrit submit-requirement referencing a specific file, or something else — all unspecified
Evidence class: Unverified — the specific enforcement surface is unstated and cannot be confirmed from plan text or repo inspection
findings.finding: AC3 asserts 'A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required)' but names no concrete mechanism. The most natural candidate (CODEOWNERS / Gerrit code-owners plugin) is explicitly rejected by ADR-0038. No per-file ACL exists in infra/gerrit/project.config. A CI-script check on the limit value is self-defeating (the CI script can be changed via a normal change). The plan does not name how this is implemented, making it unverifiable whether the capability exists as described — a critical gap before Phase 2 commits.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required); documented'
scenarios: Phase 2 executor invents an ad-hoc mechanism (e.g., grep in CI for the literal '800' constant) that is trivially bypassed or inconsistently applied
The 'documented' requirement in AC3 is satisfied with prose but the gate itself is absent, leaving the limit changeable via a normal Gerrit change
A future contributor raises the limit by editing the test or gate script, which itself goes through the normal LLM-Review+Verified flow with no special barrier

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten AC #1 to 'no longer exists' only (drop 'or is empty') so it aligns with AC #2's requirement for full removal of the mechanism.
evidence: AC #1: '.github/module-size-allowlist.txt no longer exists (or is empty)'
AC #2: 'The allowlist/ceilings mechanism (both files … ) is removed'
Goal: 'remove the allowlist/ceilings escape-hatch mechanism entirely'
findings.finding: AC #1 says the allowlist file 'no longer exists (or is empty)', but AC #2 and the Goal both require the entire allowlist/ceilings mechanism to be 'removed'. The 'or is empty' option in AC #1 would allow the file to survive with zero entries — satisfying AC #1 while violating AC #2 and the Goal's 'no module can ever be exempted again' constraint.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #1 vs AC #2 and Goal section
scenarios: An implementer leaves an empty allowlist file in place after Phase 2; AC #1 passes but AC #2 fails, leaving reviewers disagreeing on whether the story is done.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC #1 to: '.github/module-size-allowlist.txt no longer exists and no src/rebar/**/*.py file exceeds 800 LOC (verify: ...)'

evidence: verify command `find src/rebar -name '*.py' | xargs wc -l | awk '$1>800')` inspects output with `find` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable

AC item 'Every child story landed on main via Gerrit (LLM-Review +1 AND Verified +1)' cites operational evidence (merge_gate) that lives outside the codebase but is not tagged [operator-attested]; prefix the checkbox text with [operator-attested] so the completion verifier accepts a recorded attestation instead of failing to find code proof (ADR-0043).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the limit-lock gate in the plan (e.g., 'a CODEOWNERS entry requiring admin approval on the file/constant that defines the 800-LOC threshold, documented in docs/architecture.md') so the executor makes no design choice.
evidence: 'A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required); documented' — no mechanism is named
Phase 2 description says only 'add the limit-lock gate', without specifying what that gate is
findings.finding: The 'hard gate' mechanism that blocks changing the 800-LOC limit through the normal contribution process is never specified. An executor must independently choose among several incompatible approaches (CODEOWNERS on the file holding the constant, a CI script that asserts the limit equals exactly 800, branch-protection rules, a protected config file, etc.) with no guidance — a genuine design decision deferred to the executor.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required); documented'
scenarios: Executor implements a CODEOWNERS-based review gate; reviewer expected a CI assertion — both satisfy the AC literally but have different properties and reviewability standards
Executor chooses a branch-protection rule that is invisible in the repo diff, so the 'documented' requirement has no obvious target
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'The limit-lock gate is implemented as a CODEOWNERS rule requiring the `@rebar-admins` team's approval on any change to the file(s) that define the 800-LOC constant (e.g., tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py and any config file), plus a line in docs/architecture.md explaining that the limit is admin-controlled.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the Phase 2 scope to explicitly rewrite the architecture.md section describing the escape-hatch mechanism (not just prune the offender table) so it reflects the new absolute 800-LOC cap with no exemptions.
evidence: 'The module-size soft cap (800 LOC, docs/architecture.md) has a grandfathering escape hatch' — this prose in architecture.md is not scheduled for removal/rewrite, only the table is
Phase 2 AC: 'prune the architecture.md offender table' — table only, not the escape-hatch description section
findings.finding: The plan says architecture.md describes the 800-LOC soft cap with an escape-hatch mechanism; Phase 2 makes the cap absolute, but the plan only says to 'prune the offender table' — it does not say the escape-hatch prose in architecture.md will be rewritten to reflect the new absolute-cap contract, leaving stale architecture documentation.
findings.location: Phase 2 description; 'prune the architecture.md offender table'
scenarios: After Phase 2 lands, architecture.md still describes an allowlist escape hatch that no longer exists, contradicting the new absolute-cap policy
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Phase 2: 'Rewrite the module-size section of docs/architecture.md to remove all escape-hatch language and state the 800-LOC limit is now absolute, enforced by CI with no allowlist mechanism.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verify command to AC #1 that asserts the allowlist file does not exist (e.g., `[ ! -f .github/module-size-allowlist.txt ]`) alongside the wc-l check.
evidence: AC #1 text names two things: file non-existence AND no over-cap .py files
Verify step: 'find src/rebar -name '*.py' | xargs wc -l | awk \'$1>800\''  — covers only the second condition
findings.finding: AC #1's verify command (`find src/rebar -name '*.py' | xargs wc -l | awk '$1>800'`) only checks that no Python file exceeds 800 LOC — it does not verify that the allowlist file itself has been removed, which is the other half of the same criterion.
scenarios: CI verify step passes even if the allowlist file still exists but happens to be empty, masking an incomplete cleanup.
findings.suggested_fix: AC #1 verify: `[ ! -f .github/module-size-allowlist.txt ] && [ ! -f .github/module-size-ceilings.txt ] && ! find src/rebar -name '*.py' | xargs wc -l | awk '$1>800{found=1}END{exit found}'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC verifying that architecture.md's offender/remedy table is empty (or removed) after Phase 2 completes.
evidence: Phase 1: 'graduate the file off BOTH allowlist + ceilings + its architecture.md remedy row'
Phase 2: 'prune the architecture.md offender table'
No AC mentions architecture.md
findings.finding: The plan body describes pruning the architecture.md offender/remedy table in both Phase 1 ("graduate the file off … its architecture.md remedy row") and Phase 2 ("prune the architecture.md offender table"), but no Acceptance Criterion covers this deliverable.
findings.location: Decomposition — Phase 1 and Phase 2 descriptions; Acceptance Criteria (absence)
scenarios: The architecture.md table retains stale offender rows after all files are split and the mechanism is deleted, leaving documentation out of sync with reality.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] architecture.md contains no offender-table rows for over-cap modules (the table is empty or the section is removed).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a doc-sweep step to Phase 2 that explicitly identifies and updates/removes all references to `module-size-allowlist.txt` and `module-size-ceilings.txt` in CONTRIBUTING.md, README, and any other docs, not only architecture.md.
evidence: Phase 2 says 'prune the architecture.md offender table' but makes no mention of CONTRIBUTING.md, README, or any other documentation that may describe the allowlist/ceilings workflow
ABSENCE: no sweep of doc references to module-size-allowlist.txt or module-size-ceilings.txt outside architecture.md is mentioned
findings.finding: The plan removes the allowlist/ceilings mechanism and its CI shell-gate branch, but does not address whether CONTRIBUTING.md, README, or other docs that describe the allowlist workflow will be updated or pruned, potentially stranding stale references.
scenarios: A contributor reads CONTRIBUTING.md after Phase 2 lands, finds instructions for adding a file to module-size-allowlist.txt that no longer exists, and is confused or wastes time

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the documentation target for the limit-lock gate explicitly (e.g., 'document in docs/architecture.md under a new "Limit governance" section') rather than leaving 'documented' unqualified.
evidence: The word 'documented' appears without a target location
The plan references docs/architecture.md for the cap itself and tests for the contract, but no documentation target is specified for the gate
findings.finding: AC3 requires the gate to be 'documented' but does not state where the documentation must live (architecture.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, a new ADR, inline code comment). This is a defaultable gap — the executor must guess the right target.
scenarios: Executor adds one sentence to a commit message and considers it 'documented'; reviewer expected an architecture.md section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'documented' with 'documented in docs/architecture.md (new "Limit governance" subsection)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Standardise the architecture.md table name to a single term (e.g., 'offender table') in both Phase 1 and Phase 2 descriptions.
evidence: Phase 1: 'its architecture.md remedy row'
Phase 2: 'prune the architecture.md offender table'
findings.finding: The plan uses two different names for the same architecture.md artifact: 'remedy row' (Phase 1) and 'offender table' (Phase 2), creating terminology drift that could confuse implementers about whether these refer to the same structure.
scenarios: An implementer removes the 'offender table' section in Phase 2 but does not realise that individual 'remedy rows' in Phase 1 refer to rows of that same table, causing partial cleanup or double-counting work.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'its architecture.md remedy row' in Phase 1 with 'its row in the architecture.md offender table'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for Phase 2 (e.g., 'revert the Phase-2 Gerrit change' or 'restore files from git history') so the team knows how to recover if the replacement gate is defective after merge.
evidence: 'delete the allowlist+ceilings mechanism, replace the contract test with an absolute \'nothing over 800\' test' — stated as a single landing, no revert path mentioned
ABSENCE: no rollback / revert / back-out procedure anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: Phase 2 removes the allowlist/ceilings mechanism and the contract test irreversibly, but no rollback or back-out plan is stated. If the replacement absolute-cap test has a bug, or an edge-case file is missed, there is no stated revert strategy.
scenarios: Replacement test has a latent bug and lets over-800-LOC files through undetected
An emergency file temporarily needs to exceed 800 LOC with no escape valve and no documented path to temporarily relax the gate
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection under Phase 2: 'If the absolute-cap gate proves defective post-merge, revert the Phase-2 Gerrit change via `git revert <SHA>` and re-open the child story. The allowlist files are preserved in git history and can be restored in one commit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the 11 missing Phase 1 child stories (one per remaining grandfathered module) to the sibling roster so the union of children covers all 12 allowlist entries.
evidence: Parent AC1: '.github/module-size-allowlist.txt no longer exists (or is empty) and no src/rebar/**/*.py file exceeds 800 LOC'
.github/module-size-allowlist.txt lists 12 grandfathered files; child covers only inbound_differ.py
Complete sibling roster contains only one child: a130-2b7c-429c-43d1
Parent decomposition: 'Phase 1 — one child story per grandfathered module (12)'; roster shows only 1 of 12
Scope-narrowing SC-contradiction pattern: child covers 1/12 files, does not name the uncovered remainder
container pairing: parent + a130-2b7c-429c-43d1
findings.finding: Parent AC1 is only partially covered: the child graduates inbound_differ.py (1 of 12 grandfathered files) but the other 11 files (reconcile.py, config.py, det_floor.py, passes.py, lint_refs.py, gate_dispatch.py, runner.py, outbound_differ.py, dispatch_one.py, fsck.py, _processors.py) have no sibling in the complete roster. The parent criterion requires the allowlist to be empty and NO src/rebar/**/*.py file to exceed 800 LOC — this child covers a narrower scope than the criterion and names no remainder.
findings.location: child a130-2b7c-429c-43d1 — parent AC1 vs. complete sibling roster
scenarios: Completion verifier checks parent AC1 after this child lands: allowlist still has 11 rows, AC1 fails.
Phase 2 child (if it ever appears) would attempt to delete the mechanism files while 11 rows remain, contradicting its own precondition of 'drain to zero'.
findings.suggested_fix: Create 11 additional child stories mirroring this one's structure, one per remaining grandfathered file (reconcile.py, config.py, det_floor.py, passes.py, lint_refs.py, gate_dispatch.py, runner.py, outbound_differ.py, dispatch_one.py, fsck.py, _processors.py), and add them to the parent's decomposition roster.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Create the Phase 2 child story that deletes the allowlist/ceilings files, replaces the contract test with an absolute check, and prunes the shell gate's allowlist branch.
evidence: Parent AC2 requires deleting .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt, replacing test_module_size_contract.py with an absolute 'nothing over 800' test, and pruning the shell gate's allowlist branch.
Child only removes one row from each file; it explicitly does NOT delete the files or replace the contract test.
Parent decomposition: 'Phase 2 — one child story: delete the allowlist+ceilings mechanism...' — no Phase 2 child appears in the complete sibling roster.
Owned-by-none gap: no sibling in the roster owns Phase 2 work.
container pairing: parent + a130-2b7c-429c-43d1
findings.finding: Parent AC2 ('The allowlist/ceilings mechanism — both files + the sync contract test + the shell gate's allowlist branch — is removed; CI still fails any new over-cap file') is uncovered by the entire sibling roster. No child plans to delete the mechanism files, replace the contract test with an absolute check, or prune the shell gate's allowlist branch.
findings.location: child a130-2b7c-429c-43d1 — parent AC2 vs. complete sibling roster
scenarios: After all Phase 1 splits land, the allowlist/ceilings mechanism files still exist (empty) and the escape hatch is still operative.
New contributors could re-add a row to the allowlist without a hard gate blocking them.
The completion verifier tests AC2 and finds it unaddressed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Phase 2 child story to the parent's decomposition that implements: delete .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt; replace test_module_size_contract.py with a test asserting no src/rebar/**/*.py file exceeds 800 LOC (no allowlist); remove the allowlist branch from the gerrit-verify.yaml and test.yml shell gates.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the limit-lock gate (e.g., CODEOWNERS + branch-protection rule requiring admin override for the SOFT_CAP constant) and its documentation to the Phase 2 child story.
evidence: Parent AC3: 'A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required); documented'
No child in the complete sibling roster mentions the limit-lock gate.
Parent decomposition assigns this to Phase 2 ('add the limit-lock gate'), but Phase 2 child is absent from the roster.
Owned-by-none gap confirmed: neither this child nor any sibling covers AC3.
container pairing: parent + a130-2b7c-429c-43d1
findings.finding: Parent AC3 ('A hard gate blocks changing the 800 limit through the normal contribution process (admin manual override required); documented') is uncovered by the entire sibling roster. No child addresses the limit-lock gate or its documentation.
findings.location: child a130-2b7c-429c-43d1 — parent AC3 vs. complete sibling roster
scenarios: After all Phase 1 and (hypothetical) Phase 2 work lands, anyone with normal contribution access can raise SOFT_CAP in test_module_size_contract.py or the CI gate without admin approval.
The completion verifier tests AC3 and finds no gate in place.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Phase 2 child, add: (a) a CODEOWNERS or protected-path rule requiring admin approval for changes to SOFT_CAP in test_module_size_contract.py and the CI gate threshold; (b) a docs/architecture.md section documenting the override process.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC6 to verify ALL moved symbols are accessible as inbound_differ attributes, not just the 4 named ones (add _JIRA_TO_LOCAL_TYPE, _JIRA_TO_LOCAL_PRIORITY, _REBAR_STATUS_LABEL_TO_LOCAL, _extract_jira_field_value, _load_adf to the spot-check).
evidence: Child AC6 verifies: inbound_differ._map_jira_to_local_fields, inbound_differ._JIRA_TO_LOCAL_STATUS, inbound_differ._assignee_matches, inbound_differ._normalize_jira_body
_normalize_jira_body is in the moved cluster and IS in AC6 — covered.
However, _load_adf, _ADF_KEY_INBOUND, _AdfModule_Inbound, _JIRA_TO_LOCAL_TYPE, _JIRA_TO_LOCAL_PRIORITY, _REBAR_STATUS_LABEL_TO_LOCAL, _extract_jira_field_value are also moved but not verified by AC6.
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_config.py line 191: _load_attr('inbound_differ.py', '_JIRA_TO_LOCAL_STATUS') — covered by AC6.
reconcile_check.py accesses _EXCLUDED_PREFIXES which STAYS — safe, not an issue.
The 'What' section says 'imports these back... and RE-EXPORTS them' (all moved symbols), but AC6 only spot-checks 4 of the 8+ moved symbols.
container pairing: parent + a130-2b7c-429c-43d1
findings.finding: The child's backward-compat acceptance criterion (AC6) enumerates only 4 re-exported symbols for verification, but several other moved symbols are also accessed externally. Specifically, _normalize_jira_body (moved cluster) is called in tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/mutate/test_inbound_comments_diff.py and tests/integration/test_reconcile_live_e2e.py through the loaded module; _load_adf and _ADF_KEY_INBOUND (also moved) underpin stayed code's description comparison. AC6's verify command does not catch a missing re-export of these symbols.
findings.location: child a130-2b7c-429c-43d1 — AC6 backward-compat re-export list
scenarios: A future consumer or test that accesses inbound_differ._JIRA_TO_LOCAL_TYPE or inbound_differ._REBAR_STATUS_LABEL_TO_LOCAL via the module attribute would fail if the implementer missed re-exporting those symbols.
The AC6 verify command passes even if only the 4 named symbols are re-exported, leaving others silently missing.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a verify step that enumerates all moved symbols and asserts each is reachable as `getattr(inbound_differ, sym)` for the full moved-cluster list defined in the 'What' section.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
